job_statement: When I'm screening 20+ designer portfolios for a mid-level role, I want to quickly determine if this person has shipped real product and can explain their decisions, so I can confidently put them in the 'yes' pile for a phone screen.
context: A hiring manager has a stack of portfolios sent by a recruiter or sourced on LinkedIn. They have maybe 20 minutes to cut the list from 20 to 5. Each portfolio gets 45–90 seconds before a decision is made. The candidate is not present to explain anything — the portfolio has to do the work alone.
current_solutions: LinkedIn profiles with featured work sections
Dribbble or Behance for visual work
PDF case study decks shared over email
Take-home challenge to supplement the portfolio review
desired_outcome: In under 2 minutes, the hiring manager can answer: Does this person do real research? Can they ship? Do they understand the problem they were solving? If yes to all three, they move forward.
portfolio_implications: The homepage must communicate seniority and type of work in the first scroll — no burying the lead
Case study titles need to signal outcomes, not just project names
The first paragraph of each case study must answer: what was the problem and what did Rob own?
Outcomes need numbers — SUS scores, views, funding, acquisition — not adjectives
Process must be scannable — headers, visuals, and callouts for key decisions
The site itself must look professional — a poorly designed portfolio disqualifies before the work is read
